causal_species: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides (mango); C. truncatum, C. scovillei, C. siamense complex (chilli)

notes.en: Warm, humid, rainy conditions favour infection. Spores spread by rain splash. Latent infection on immature fruit expresses at ripening — a key post-harvest threat in mango.

description.en: In mango: dark, water-soaked lesions on stems, leaves, flowers and fruits; immature leaves and flowers are especially susceptible; *C. gloeosporioides* is the main pathogen across Sri Lankan cultivated regions. In chilli: sunken necrotic lesions on fruit with concentric rings of acervuli; 260+ *Colletotrichum* isolates have been recovered from chilli in a multi-country Asian survey including Sri Lanka. Management: resistant varieties, nursery sanitation (avoid Solanaceae rotation history), well-drained sites, pre/post-harvest fungicide schedules, and post-harvest hot-water treatment for mango.

description.quote: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides spreads across cultivated regions of Sri Lanka and can infect flowers, immature leaves, mature leaves, stems and fruits. Infected plants develop dark, water soaked lesions on stems, leaves and fruits, with immature leaves and flowers being highly susceptible.

favored_conditions.quote: Typical anthracnose symptoms on chilli fruit include sunken necrotic tissues, with concentric rings of acervuli.

notes.en: Warm temperatures (25–34 °C), high humidity, strong winds and continuous heavy rains, and excessive nitrogen fertilization favor spread.

description.en: At the seedling stage causes wilting, yellowing, and systemic 'kresek'; squeezing cut ends of affected leaves releases yellowish bacterial ooze. In mature plants, water-soaked orange stripes develop on blades or leaf tips, with wavy-margined lesions progressing toward the base.

description.quote: Seedling stage: wilting and yellowing of leaves, or wilting of seedlings (called kresek) with yellowish bacterial ooze coming out of the cut ends when leaves squeezed. Mature plants: lesions usually develop as water-soaked orange stripes on leaf blades or leaf tips. Lesions have a wavy margin and progress toward the leaf base.

favored_conditions.quote: temperatures at 25−34°C, strong winds and continuous heavy rains, High humidity, Excessive application of nitrogen fertilizer

notes.en: Wet, warm conditions and continuous cropping with solanaceous plants build up soil inoculum. Wounds during transplanting or cultivation provide entry points.

description.en: A devastating soil-borne bacterial disease. Rapid wilting of entire plant without yellowing; cut stems ooze milky-white bacterial slime in clean water. Causes 20–60% yield loss in brinjal and up to 90% in tomato in Sri Lanka. No chemical control; managed through resistant varieties (e.g., Lanka Sour rootstock for grafting HORDI Tomato hybrid 03), grafting, biological control (Trichoderma asperellum, Bordeaux mixture), crop rotation with non-hosts, and field sanitation.

causal_species.quote: Bacterial wilt is the primary concern. There is no chemical control for this disease

description.quote: Bacterial wilt caused by Ralstonia solanacearum is a major soil-borne disease of brinjal cultivation in Sri Lanka, with overall yield losses ranging from 20-60%. For tomato, bacterial wilt is one of the most devastating diseases causing up to 90% of yield loss in Sri Lanka.

favored_conditions.quote: Tomato bacterial wilt is successfully managed through usage of resistant cultivars, grafting, biological control agents

causal_species: Banana bunchy top virus (Nanoviridae); Sri Lanka (Kandy) isolate classified in Pacific Indian Oceans (PIO) group

notes.en: Aphid vector requires ≥4 h feeding on infected plants to acquire, ≥15 min feeding on healthy plants to transmit. Persistent, circulative, non-propagative transmission.

description.en: Sri Lankan isolate characterised from Kandy (BBTV-K). Infected banana plants develop a characteristic 'bunchy top' — severely shortened petioles crowded into a rosette at the pseudostem apex, narrow chlorotic-margin leaves with dark-green 'dot-and-dash' streaks on midrib and petiole. No fruit produced from infected mats. Spread by **banana aphid *Pentalonia nigronervosa*** and by infected suckers. **Management**: (a) strict phytosanitary rogueing — destroy infected mats immediately; (b) imidacloprid injection or paraffinic-oil spray of the infested plant plus all plants within 10 m; (c) plant only BBTV-free tissue-cultured or certified suckers; (d) control aphid populations in nursery and field; (e) isolate planting areas from old infected stands.

causal_species.quote: Six genomic DNA components of a BBTV isolate from Kandy, Sri Lanka (BBTV-K). Phylogenetic analysis grouped the Sri Lanka (Kandy) isolate in the Pacific Indian Oceans (PIO) group

description.quote: BBTV is transmitted by banana aphid Pentalonia nigronervosa Coquerel in a persistent, circulative and non-propagative manner. Aphids require a feeding period of at least 4 hours or more on diseased plants to become infective, and a minimum feeding period of 15 minutes on susceptible plants to transmit the virus. The most important factors in controlling BBTV are killing the aphid vector and rogueing (removing and destroying) infected banana plants. Banana aphids can be treated by either injecting the infested banana plant with imidacloprid or by spraying with paraffinic oil

names.en: Black Sigatoka (black leaf streak of banana)

notes.en: Warm, humid, frequently wet banana canopies favour ascospore release and infection. Dense plantations amplify inoculum rapidly.

description.en: The **most significant foliar disease of banana worldwide**. Thin dark streaks parallel to leaf veins expand into dark brown/black oval lesions with yellow halos that coalesce and prematurely kill leaves, reducing bunch weight and triggering early ripening. No effective host resistance in Cavendish. Commercial plantations control with up to **70 fungicide applications per year** — a protectant rotation of mancozeb (up to 35 applications/yr) alternated with 14–20 applications of systemics from triazole, strobilurin, morpholine, and benzimidazole groups. Weekly applications drive rapid resistance evolution (especially in 14α-demethylase inhibitor / DMI group). Fungicide cost is 10–15% of total banana production cost in major exporting countries. Urgent need for alternatives: defence-inducer sprays, resistant breeding lines, and IPM.

description.quote: Black Sigatoka (black leaf streak disease) is caused by the fungus Pseudocercospora fijiensis (previously: Mycosphaerella fijiensis) and is the most significant foliar disease of banana worldwide. fungicide treatments, which are applied up to 70 times per year and are estimated to comprise 10 to 15 percent of the total production cost of banana in Costa Rica. Intensive management involves applying a protectant fungicide (mancozeb) up to 35 times per year with an alternation of 14–20 applications of systemic fungicides using preferably triazoles, strobilurins, morpholines and in some cases benzimidazoles.

notes.en: Broad temperature range 16–36 °C, humidity 86–100 %, nutrient-deficient or problem soils (high salinity, iron toxicity), and drought stress promote infection.

description.en: Circular to oval spots on leaves and seeds, ranging from minute dark specks to large dark-brown/reddish-brown ovals. Seedling blight weakens stands; grain infection produces lightweight or chalky kernels. Historically linked to the Bengal famine of 1943.

description.quote: minute dark (dark brown to reddish brown) spots to large oval to circular spots. Seedling blight may result in weak plant stands, while grain infection can produce lightweight or chalky kernels.

favored_conditions.quote: Environmental temperatures between 16-36°C, High humidity (86-100%), Nutrient-deficient or problem soils (high salinity, iron toxicity), Drought stress

names.en: Cinnamon leaf spot / Grey leaf blight

causal_species: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides (dark leaf spot/blight) + Pestalotia cinnamomi (grey leaf spot)

notes.en: Incidence significantly higher in **lands with high shade, high planting densities, poor weeding, and improper pruning** — all canopy/management factors that raise humidity and slow leaf drying.

description.en: Two principal foliar diseases co-documented on Sri Lankan cinnamon: **leaf spot / blight** (*Colletotrichum gloeosporioides* — same pathogen as mango anthracnose) and **grey leaf spot / blight** (*Pestalotia cinnamomi*). Lesions reduce bark-yield quality in the short term and weaken bushes over successive harvests. **Management**: (a) **optimal shade** (not excessive); (b) **appropriate planting density** per DEA recommendations — avoid over-dense stands; (c) regular weeding and proper pruning to improve air flow; (d) prompt removal of severely infected leaves; (e) copper-based fungicide sprays (e.g. copper oxychloride) at early-symptom stage.

causal_species.quote: The important diseases of cinnamon reported are leaf spot/blight caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides and grey leaf spot/blight caused by Pestalotia cinnamomi

favored_conditions.quote: Considerably higher disease incidence was observed in lands with high shades, high planting densities, poor weeding, and improper pruning

notes.en: Prevalent on ill-drained soils — waterlogging drives zoospore spread to young bark. Heavy shade + high planting density + poor weeding amplify disease.

description.en: **Important disease affecting cinnamon stems in Sri Lanka** — the country's flagship spice crop. Symptoms: vertical stripes of dead bark, most numerous near ground level; up to **42% bark damage** in infested plantations. **Management per TARE review**: (a) **well-drained soils** to minimise disease incidence; (b) phytosanitation — remove and destroy affected parts, dress wounds with tar; (c) **soil application of sulphur**; (d) reduce excessive shade and dense planting; (e) proper pruning and weeding; (f) select tolerant accessions from ongoing DEA cinnamon germplasm trials.

causal_species.quote: Phytopthora cinnamomi causes stripe canker, which is an important disease affecting cinnamon stems in Sri Lanka

description.quote: Vertical stripes of dead bark are most numerous near ground level, and the disease is prevalent on ill-drained soils and causes up to 42% bark damage. For stripe canker management, well drainage is important to minimize disease incidence at a lower level, and phytosanitation such as removal and destruction of affected parts and wound dressing with tar have been recommended as management measures. Additionally, soil application of sulphur has been recommended for the control of this disease.

causal_species: Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus (CLas) — phloem-limited α-proteobacterium; also CLaf and CLam variants

notes.en: Spread by the Asian citrus psyllid (*Diaphorina citri*) and also via infected grafting material. Warm temperate-to-tropical citrus zones sustain both vector and pathogen year-round.

description.en: One of the most devastating diseases of citrus globally — has collapsed commercial citrus industries in Florida and elsewhere. Symptoms: asymmetric blotchy-mottle leaf chlorosis (distinctive from nutrient deficiencies which are symmetric), yellow shoots, lopsided bitter fruits that fail to colour, progressive tree decline. **No known cure** — infected trees are difficult to regenerate and study. CBID management follows a triangular strategy — target pathogen, host, vector: (a) **cultivate pathogen-free certified seedlings** (foundational); (b) **aggressively remove infected trees** to reduce inoculum; (c) **control Asian citrus psyllid** with rotating insecticide classes + biocontrol wasp *Tamarixia radiata*; (d) emerging: thermotherapy, chemotherapy, plant-defence activators, nano-emulsions; (e) use-of antibiotics restricted in most regulatory regimes. For Sri Lanka's Citrus Research Station (Bibile), early monitoring + certified-seedling programmes are the primary line of defence.

causal_species.quote: Three species of bacterium are known to be associated with citrus greening disease: Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus (CLas), Candidatus Liberibacter africanus (CLaf), and Candidatus Liberibacter americanus (CLam)

description.quote: Huanglongbing (HLB, aka citrus greening) is one of the most devastating diseases of citrus, caused by the phloem-limited bacteria Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus (CLas), which are transmitted via psyllid. To date, there are no effective long-term commercialized control measures for HLB. A triangular disease management approach has been introduced targeting pathogen, host, and vector. Currently, it is essential for the prevention and control of HLB to use antibiotics and pesticides while reducing the spread of HLB by cultivating pathogen-free seedlings, removing disease trees, and killing Asian citrus psyllid (ACP).

causal_species: Plasmodiophora brassicae (obligate biotrophic protist — sometimes called 'fungus-like'; schema enum 'fungal' used pending schema extension)

notes.en: **Acidic soils (pH <6.5) of Sri Lanka's highland brassica areas (elevation 1,250–2,250 m — Nuwara Eliya, Badulla) drive incidence.** Resting spores can survive 15+ years in soil. Waterlogged soils aid zoospore mobility.

description.en: Reported in Sri Lanka since 1975, now a significant production constraint in highland brassica cultivation (Nuwara Eliya, Badulla, Welimada) where elevation is 1,250–2,250 m. Forms swollen club-shaped galls on roots — infected plants wilt in daytime, stunt, and fail to form heads. **Management**: (a) **liming** to raise soil pH above 7 (DOA HORDI recommendation for knol-khol is lifting pH to 7.1–7.2 with agricultural lime); (b) **crop rotation** with non-brassicas for 5–7 years; (c) **resistant cultivars** where available; (d) **drainage improvement** — raised beds in wet soils; (e) **biological** — *Bacillus subtilis* QST713 (Serenade) soil drench reduces severity 62–83% in canola trials; (f) sanitise tools and footwear moving between fields.

description.quote: Sri Lanka is believed to have reported clubroot disease in 1975, and it remains a significant agricultural problem in the country. In Sri Lanka, crucifer crops including cabbage are important vegetables, and the incidence of clubroot is high in the hill areas at an elevation between 1250 and 2250 m a.s.l. The acidic soils of the highlands contribute to the prevalence of clubroot in the region.

favored_conditions.quote: Serenade, a biofungicide consisting of Bacillus subtilis QST713, when applied as a soil drench, has been reported to reduce the clubroot severity in canola by 62–83%

notes.en: Warm humid conditions with leaf wetness of ≥12 h drive infection. Dense coffee canopies and shade plantations (mid-range humidity) accelerate build-up.

description.en: **The disease that ended Ceylon's coffee industry** — first observed in Sri Lanka in 1869 at Madulsima as orange pustules on coffee leaves. By the 1880s it had destroyed over 90% of the country's coffee plantations, prompting the pivot to tea that defines Sri Lankan agriculture today. Symptoms: orange-yellow powdery uredospore pustules on the underside of leaves; heavy defoliation weakens plants and reduces yield by 30–50%. The disease remains a yield-limiting factor in the modern small-scale arabica revival. Management: (a) **resistant cultivars** (Catimor, Castillo, Sarchimor lines bred for CLR resistance); (b) preventive copper fungicide sprays at early rain onset; (c) systemic triazoles + strobilurins in high-pressure seasons; (d) shade and canopy management to reduce humidity; (e) prompt removal of infected leaves; (f) balanced fertilisation (excess N worsens susceptibility).

description.quote: coffee plantations were devastated by the fungal disease Hemileia vastatrix, also known as coffee leaf rust (CLR). The coffee leaf rust fungus, Hemileia vastatrix, was first detected in Ceylon in 1869, initially observed as orange pustules on coffee leaves in the Madulsima region. leaf rust persists as a yield-limiting factor, capable of reducing output by 30-50% through defoliation and weakened plants.

scientific_name: Pythium spp. / Rhizoctonia solani / Phytophthora spp.
causal_species: Pythium spp. (primary), Rhizoctonia solani, Phytophthora spp., Fusarium spp.

notes.en: Wet, poorly drained nursery beds, cool-to-moderate temperatures, dense seedling stands, and excessive irrigation all favour outbreaks. Can cause 60% seedling loss in solanaceous crops.

description.en: Death of seedlings either before emergence (pre-emergence damping-off: seed and seedling rot before soil surface) or shortly after (post-emergence: young seedlings fall over with dark, shrivelled stem at the soil line). Primary threat in nursery beds for solanaceous vegetables (tomato, brinjal, chilli, capsicum) and brassicas. Management: (a) soil solarisation — cover moist nursery beds with 250-gauge polythene for 30 days pre-sowing; (b) Trichoderma viride 1.2 kg/ha soil application; (c) seed treatment with Captan 50% WP at 4 g/kg seed or Thiophanate-methyl 50% + Thiram 30% WP at 4 g/kg; (d) Thiophanate-methyl 70% WP as nursery soil drench; (e) raised beds with good drainage; minimise irrigation; avoid dense seeding.

causal_species.quote: The disease is caused by Phytophthora spp., Pythium spp., and Rhizoctonia spp.

description.quote: About 60% losses at seedling stage in both nursery and field level have been reported due to damping off disease in tomato, chilli and brinjal caused by Pythium species. Seed treatment options include Captan 50% WP (4 g/Kg of seeds) and Thiophanate Methyl; 50%+Thiram 30% WP (4 g/kg of seeds). Soil solarization by spreading 250 gauge polythene sheet over the bed for 30 days before sowing and application of bio-control agent Trichoderma viride in soil @ 1.2kg/ha is found effective

notes.en: Infects >40 species across 20 genera of Cucurbitaceae. Obligate biotroph — requires living tissue. Sporangia form between 5 and 30 °C on cucumber. Leaf wetness from rain or heavy dew drives infection.

description.en: Yellow, angular chlorotic lesions on upper leaf surface limited by leaf veins, sometimes with necrotic centres; dark greyish-purple sporulation visible on the lower leaf surface in humid conditions. Rapid defoliation under pressure. Since 2004, global host resistance has broken down in most cucurbit cultivars — management now requires intensive fungicide programmes with resistance management (rotate MoAs: Oxathiapiprolin, mandipropamid, cymoxanil, mancozeb, chlorothalonil). Early detection by field scouting is critical. Confusable with powdery mildew but distinguished by sporulation colour and leaf position — downy mildew shows on leaf underside, powdery mildew on the upper surface.

description.quote: Pseudoperonospora cubensis is an obligate biotrophic pathogen that infects over 40 host plant species belonging to 20 genera of the family Cucurbitaceae. The optimum temperature for sporangia production is 15–20 °C, but sporangia may form on cucumber at temperatures from 5 to 30 °C.

favored_conditions.quote: Since the 2004 growing season, host resistance has been effective no longer and the control of downy mildew on cucurbits now requires an intensive fungicide programme

causal_species: Alternaria solani (tomato), A. linariae, A. tomatophila

notes.en: Warm, humid, frequently wet foliage (dew) drives infection. Stressed plants (water, nutrient) are most susceptible.

description.en: The most destructive tomato disease in tropical and subtropical regions; each 1% severity increase reduces yield by ~1.36%, with complete loss possible under severe pressure. Distinguishing symptom: **concentric-ring lesions** ('target spots') on lower leaves that enlarge, yellow, and cause defoliation; sunscald on exposed fruit follows. On potato tubers, dark sunken lesions with corky dry rot. Management: (a) rotate out of solanaceous crops for ≥3 years; (b) resistant cultivars; (c) wider spacing / canopy management for airflow; (d) preventive fungicides — mancozeb, chlorothalonil, azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin, copper products, ziram, or *Bacillus subtilis* biologicals; (e) monitor during warm damp weather and spray before symptoms worsen.

description.quote: Early blight caused by A. solani is the most destructive disease of tomatoes in the tropical and subtropical regions. Each 1% increase in intensity can reduce yield by 1.36%, and complete crop failure can occur when the disease is most severe. Distinguishing symptoms include leaf spot and defoliation, most pronounced in the lower canopy, with lesions that resemble concentric rings. Fungicides available include azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin, Bacillus subtilis, chlorothalonil, copper products, hydrogen dioxide, mancozeb, potassium bicarbonate, and ziram.

notes.en: Waterlogged soils and poor drainage are principal drivers. Continuous ginger/turmeric cropping on the same bed amplifies inoculum.

description.en: **Responsible for 50–90% of production loss in ginger globally.** Leaves yellow and droop, pseudostems collapse, and rhizomes become water-soaked, soft, and foul-smelling. **DEA Sri Lanka management** (per the ginger page): (a) **seed treatment** — dip rhizomes in **Mancozeb 80% WP at 30 g per 1 L water for 30 minutes** before planting; (b) plant only disease-free healthy rhizomes; (c) maintain proper drainage, raised beds; (d) **soil solarisation** of beds before planting; (e) rotate — no more than two consecutive seasons of ginger/turmeric on the same plot; (f) rogue infected plants; (g) curative drench with **40 g copper oxychloride per 10 L water** OR **1% Bordeaux mixture** applied by watering can to all beds (infected + healthy); repeat after 2 weeks; (h) emerging biocontrol — *Trichoderma harzianum*, *Pseudomonas* spp., *Bacillus subtilis*, *Streptomyces* spp.

causal_species.quote: Pythium and Fusarium spp. are of most concern, causing soft rot (rhizome rot) disease, majorly responsible for the downfall in ginger production by 50–90%

description.quote: Use of healthy rhizomes in new planting, with seed treatment by dipping seed rhizomes in Mancozeb 80% WP (30g in 1 L water) solution for 30 minutes. 40 g of copper oxy chloride dissolved in 10 liters of water or 1% Bordeaux mixture should be drenched to all beds in the field (both infected and healthy) by using a watering can, repeated after two weeks

causal_species: Phytophthora infestans (Oomycete — technically a water mould, not a true fungus)

notes.en: Thrives in cool, wet, humid weather typical of Sri Lanka's up-country potato zones (Nuwara Eliya, Badulla). Sporangia spread by wind and rain splash; leaf wetness >10 h triggers infection.

description.en: Water-soaked lesions on leaves that rapidly enlarge into brown/black necrotic patches with pale green or yellow margins and white sporulation on the underside in humid conditions. On tubers, reddish-brown corky rot extends inwards; secondary soft rots follow. Historically caused the Irish Potato Famine. Control requires integrated approach: resistant cultivars, certified clean seed, crop spacing for airflow, destroying volunteer plants, and 7–10 day preventive fungicide intervals (5–7 days once disease detected). Mancozeb (protectant) is typically tank-mixed with a systemic fungicide; 10+ applications may be needed per season under pressure. Alternate modes of action to avoid resistance.

description.quote: Phytophthora infestans is one of the main pathogens in the agricultural sector, with the potato (Solanum tuberosum) being of great agricultural importance. In the case of P. infestans, mancozeb, which has a purely protective effect, is typically applied in association with a systemic fungicide. Control of the disease often requires 10 or more fungicide applications during the growing season. Before disease occurs, fungicides should be applied at 7-10 day intervals, and after disease is detected in the area, fungicides should be applied at 5-7 day intervals.

notes.en: Seed-borne and persists between seasons via infected seed and crop residue. Common and sometimes severe in major Sri Lankan rice districts.

description.en: Lesions show a chevron pattern of light (tan) and darker reddish-brown zones on mature leaves; affected foliage turns straw-coloured and dry. At grain-fill, infected florets discolour and may lead to kernel sterility or abortion.

description.quote: a chevron pattern of light (tan) and darker reddish-brown areas on mature leaves. Affected foliage becomes straw-colored and dry.

favored_conditions.quote: seed borne and survives between seasons/crops via infected seeds

causal_species: Fusarium mangiferae (primary); related species in the *Fusarium fujikuroi* complex

notes.en: Reported in Sri Lanka, India, USA, Oman, Israel, Egypt, Spain, South Africa, Malaysia, China. Mite-borne and graft-borne spread important.

description.en: Fungal disease that disrupts normal growth of mango flowers, leaves, and branches. **Vegetative malformation**: bunchy-top of small malformed leaves on short internodes; **floral malformation**: dense, branched, green and sterile panicles that fail to set fruit. *F. mangiferae* is confirmed in Sri Lanka and is the most frequent global cause of MMD. Management: (a) prune shoots carrying malformed panicles to 30–45 cm below the visible infection; (b) deblossom early/infested panicles; (c) sanitation — burn pruned material away from the orchard; (d) restrict grafting material to confirmed-clean mother trees; (e) monitor mite populations (eriophyid mites are suspected vectors).

causal_species.quote: Mango malformation is a fungal disease caused by the pathogen Fusarium moliliforme, which disrupts the normal growth of mango flowers, leaves and branches

description.quote: F. mangiferae has been discovered in Sri Lanka, the USA, Oman, India, Israel, Egypt, Spain, South Africa, Malaysia, and China, which seem to be the most frequent cause of MMD in the world

favored_conditions.quote: Remedial measures include pruning of shoots bearing malformed panicles and deblossoming of early emerged / infested panicles

notes.en: Severity varies year to year and worsens as rice approaches maturity; varietal susceptibility strongly influences development.

description.en: Light-to-dark-brown linear lesions 2–10 mm long run parallel to the leaf vein on blades and sheaths. On susceptible varieties lesions coalesce into extensive necrotic regions; a distinctive 'net blotch' pattern can develop on leaf sheaths.

description.quote: light to dark brown, linear, and progress parallel to the vein on leaf blades and sheaths, typically 2-10 mm long. On susceptible varieties, these lesions may merge to form extensive necrotic regions.

favored_conditions.quote: severity of the disease varies from year to year and is more severe as rice plants approach maturity

causal_species: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense (Foc); critical emerging strain is Tropical Race 4 (TR4)

notes.en: Soil-borne chlamydospores persist 30+ years in soil. Spreads via contaminated soil on tools, footwear, vehicles, irrigation water, and infected planting material.

description.en: Progressive yellowing and wilting of older leaves that collapse around the pseudostem; splitting of pseudostem base; internal reddish-brown vascular discoloration visible in corm cross-sections. The 1950s Race 1 strain destroyed the Gros Michel cultivar globally; since the 2010s the TR4 strain threatens Cavendish (the world's dominant export cultivar) and appears to be spreading across Asia. Control is extremely difficult — Foc TR4 is highly tolerant to azoles, SDHI, and strobilurin fungicides. Management is **phytosanitary**: quarantine to prevent introduction, strict farm hygiene, rogue-and-burn infected mats, fallow or rotate with non-host crops, plant disease-free tissue-cultured suckers, and shift to tolerant cultivars where breeding progress allows.

description.quote: Panama disease is a plant disease that infects banana plants caused by the fungus Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense. The pathogen is resistant to fungicides, and its control is limited to phytosanitary measures. Since the 2010s, a new outbreak of Panama disease caused by the strain Tropical Race 4 (TR4) has threatened the production of the Cavendish banana

favored_conditions.quote: All FocTR4 morphotypes are highly tolerant of the most widely-used single-site fungicides (azoles, succinate dehydrogenase inhibitors, strobilurins)

causal_species: Papaya ringspot virus (Potyvirus; type-P strain infects papaya, type-W cucurbits)

notes.en: Transmitted non-persistently by aphids (>20 species) and mechanically via tools and handling. Aphids acquire and transmit virus in seconds; chemical aphid control is therefore ineffective.

description.en: Papaya ring spot potyvirus strain P **confirmed present in Sri Lanka**. Symptoms on papaya: prominent **concentric ringspots and greasy streaks on fruit skin**, vein-clearing, severe mosaic with 'shoestring' leaf distortion, stunting, early fruit drop and loss of papain quality. Can cause total crop loss in commercial plantings. **Management is prevention-focused** (no cure once infected): (a) quarantine / geographic displacement of plantings away from mature infected orchards; (b) **roguing** — remove and destroy symptomatic trees immediately; (c) **netting of young plants** during early-growth susceptibility window; (d) **cross-protection** with mild PRSV strains (used in Taiwan, Hawaii); (e) **resistant cultivars** — SunUp and Rainbow (GM) in Hawaii; conventional tolerance breeding in Sri Lanka; (f) sanitation of tools between plants to prevent mechanical spread; (g) control alternate cucurbit hosts near papaya plantings.

causal_species.quote: There is documented occurrence of papaya ring spot potyvirus strain P in Sri Lanka

description.quote: There are four main methods of control for PRSV: quarantine and geographic displacement, roguing and netting, cross-protection, and genetic modification of the host plant.

favored_conditions.quote: Early detection and removal of infected plants help prevent the spread of the virus to healthy crops. Planting resistant papaya varieties, such as SunUp and Rainbow, show better resistance to PRSV. Cleaning tools, pruning equipment, and other farming implements minimizes the mechanical spread of the virus.

causal_species: Pineapple mealybug wilt-associated viruses (PMWaVs) — Ampelovirus complex in combination with mealybug feeding

notes.en: Ant associations amplify spread — ants tend mealybugs and protect them from natural enemies. Sri Lanka is among regions where *Dysmicoccus brevipes* is a serious threat to pineapple production.

description.en: Destructive parasitic complex — requires both **PMWaV virus** and **mealybug feeding** (*Dysmicoccus brevipes* pink pineapple mealybug or *D. neobrevipes* grey pineapple mealybug) to express the disease. Symptoms: leaf tips redden and curl downward, roots cease growing, plant wilts and dies within weeks. Pineapple's singular most important disease in Sri Lanka (alongside crown/root rot from *Phytophthora*). **Management**: (a) reduce mealybug populations with selective insecticides; (b) **ant control is critical** — ants shepherd mealybugs and protect them from parasitoids; (c) clean planting material (virus-free vitroplants reduce incidence dramatically); (d) exogenous salicylic acid (SA), acibenzolar-S-methyl (ASM), and methyl salicylate (MeSA) reduce mealybug populations 50–80%; (e) rogue severely affected plants.

causal_species.quote: Mealybug wilt of pineapple (MWP) is a destructive disease worldwide caused by a parasitic complex that includes Pineapple Mealybug Wilt-associated Viruses (PMWaVs) and mealybugs (Dysmicoccus brevipes), which concurrently act as vectors for these viruses

description.quote: Reducing the mealybug population is key to managing MWP. exogenous SA, ASM, and MeSA reducing the population of mealybugs by a minimum of 50% and up to 80%.

favored_conditions.quote: Closterovirus-like particles associated with mealybug wilt of pineapple were acquired and transmitted by the pink pineapple mealybug, Dysmicoccus brevipes, and the gray pineapple mealybug, D. neobrevipes. the presence of ants was correlated with an increased rate of virus spread

causal_species: Podosphaera xanthii (primary on cucurbits in Sri Lanka)

notes.en: Unlike most fungal diseases, powdery mildew thrives under warm dry weather with cool humid nights. Low to moderate relative humidity favours conidial germination; free water suppresses germination.

description.en: Whitish, powdery fungal growth on upper leaf surfaces; severe infection causes leaf yellowing, defoliation, reduced photosynthesis and yield loss. Affects all major cucurbits grown in Sri Lanka (cucumber, snake gourd, pumpkin, bitter gourd, luffa). DOA's Pest Management Recommendations (2015) list Carbendazim 50% WG, Thiophanate-methyl 70% WP, Chlorothalonil 500 g/l SC, and Potassium bicarbonate 82% SP as approved fungicides with specific dosages and pre-harvest intervals. Sulphur fungicides are not recommended for cucurbits as they can be phytotoxic in hot weather.

description.quote: Carbendazim 50% WG... Thiophanate-methyl 70% WP... Chlorothalonil 500g/l SC... Potassium bicarbonate 82% SP... sulphur-containing fungicides are not recommended for cucurbits and can be phytotoxic during hot weather
description.review_notes: Dosage specifics for each fungicide are captured in separate remedy records pending agronomist review before publication.

notes.en: Low night temperatures (17–20 °C), high humidity, excessive nitrogen fertilizer, foggy conditions, and high plant densities favor the pathogen.

description.en: The most economically important rice disease in Sri Lanka. Produces spindle-shaped leaf lesions, infects nodes (which blacken and rot), and rots the neck of the panicle causing whiteheads and grain loss. Severely infected crops can suffer major yield loss.

causal_species.review_notes: DOA page uses legacy Magnaporthe grisea; Magnaporthe oryzae is the currently accepted species for rice-infecting strains. Aliased accordingly.

description.quote: Spindle-shaped leaf spots with brown or reddish/yellowish-brown margins, ashy centers, and pointed ends. Nodes become black and rot. Panicles: rotten neck or neck rot and causes the panicle to fall off. Severe infection produces whiteheads.

favored_conditions.quote: Low temperature during night (17-20OC), High humidity, Excessive application of nitrogen fertilizer, foggy conditions, and high plant densities

scientific_name: Rice tungro bacilliform virus + Rice tungro spherical virus
causal_species: Complex of two viruses: Rice tungro bacilliform virus (RTBV, Caulimoviridae) + Rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV, Secoviridae)

notes.en: Present in Sri Lanka and across South/Southeast Asia. Transmitted semipersistently by green leafhopper *Nephotettix virescens* (highest efficiency of the vector complex).

description.en: **Confirmed present in Sri Lanka** along with Bangladesh, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Thailand. Two-virus complex — **both** must be transmitted for full disease expression. **Vector**: green leafhopper *Nephotettix virescens* (semi-persistent transmission). Symptoms: yellow-orange leaf discoloration starting from leaf tip, stunted plants, delayed maturity, few tillers, and reduced grain fill; mistaken for nutrient deficiency when mild. Management: (a) **resistant varieties** (IRRI Utri Rajapan-derived resistance genes) — most economical; (b) rogue infected plants; (c) vector control with selective insecticides; (d) synchronised planting across a landscape reduces leafhopper carry-over; (e) reduce to two rice crops per year where possible; (f) encourage natural enemies (egg parasitoid wasps, mirid bugs, strepsipterans, pipunculid flies, nematodes).

causal_species.quote: Rice tungro disease results from infection by two distinct virus species—Rice tungro bacilliform virus (RTBV) and Rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV)—which are mainly transmitted by the green leafhopper (GLH) Nephotettix virescens in a semipersistent manner. Nephotettix virescens is the most important vector species, with the highest transmission efficiency than the other vector species.

favored_conditions.quote: Association of RTBV and RTSV with tungro has been confirmed in Bangladesh, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Sri Lanka and Thailand

notes.en: Warm, humid Wet-Zone conditions favour persistence. Fungus spreads via root-to-root contact from infected stumps, decaying wood, and rhizomorphs through soil.

description.en: The most destructive root disease in rubber plantations across Sri Lanka, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and West/Central Africa. The white mycelial mat fans out over infected roots — characteristic 'boot-lace' rhizomorphs visible when the soil is scraped. Above-ground symptoms: leaves slowly lose gloss, turn yellowish then orange-brown, become leathery and curve downward, then leaf fall and die-back. Management by RRISL follows a tiered IPM strategy: (a) site preparation — remove old stumps and infected wood before replanting; (b) host-plant resistance — screen and select resistant RRIC / RRIM clones (RRIM 2002, RRIM 2024 under study with soluble-silicon supplementation); (c) **biological control** with *Trichoderma* isolates from local rubber-soil populations, antagonistic to *R. microporus*; (d) chemical trunk-paint on exposed collars; (e) rogue and destroy to interrupt rhizomorph spread.

description.quote: Symptoms on leaves of infected rubber trees develop as slightly off-green discoloration losing glossy appearance which eventually turns yellowish, mature leaves become leathery and curve downward, and at later stages, leaves become orange-brownish and fall off causing die-back

favored_conditions.quote: Trichoderma isolates obtained from rubber growing soils were found antagonistic against R. microporus

notes.en: Warm temperatures up to 40 °C, humidity above 90 %, excessive nitrogen, dense plant populations, cloudy weather and shade all favor infection.

description.en: Lesions develop near the waterline on leaf sheaths — 1–3 cm, white-centred with green, brown and orange banding. Sclerotia (4–5 mm) form on leaf surfaces, initially white then turning brown or purplish-brown at maturity.

description.quote: Lesions develop near the water level on leaf sheaths, measuring 1-3 cm with white in the center, banded with green, brown, and orange coloration. Sclerotia (4-5 mm structures) form on leaf surfaces, initially white but turning brown or purplish brown at maturity.

favored_conditions.quote: high temperature conditions reaching 40°C, moisture levels exceeding 90%, excessive nitrogen application, and dense plant populations. Cloudy weather and shade also promote infection.

notes.en: Temperatures 20–28 °C during flowering-to-fruiting, elevated moisture, and excessive nitrogen application promote outbreaks.

description.en: Oblong or irregular oval lesions with gray or light-brown centres and dark reddish-brown diffuse margins appear on the flag-leaf sheath. In severe cases a powdery white growth of spores and hyphae covers affected tissue and panicle emergence is impaired.

description.quote: Lesions are oblong or irregular oval spots with gray or light-brown centers and a dark reddish-brown, diffuse margin. A powdery white growth consisting of spores and hyphae

favored_conditions.quote: temperatures ranging from 20-28°C, elevated moisture levels, and excessive nitrogen application

causal_species: Sri Lankan cassava mosaic virus (Geminiviridae: Begomovirus)

notes.en: Spread by infected stem cuttings (primary in Sri Lanka) and by whitefly *Bemisia tabaci*. Only the 'Asia II 1' cryptic whitefly species transmits SLCMV efficiently among three Asian biotypes.

description.en: Named for its first confirmed description in Sri Lanka; now spreading across South-East Asia (detected in Cambodia, Vietnam 2015; Laos 2020). Infected plants show leaf curl and mosaic patterning on young leaves, stunting, and yield reduction. Can be systemic or localised. Management depends on (a) planting only virus-tested, disease-free stem cuttings, (b) roguing infected plants, (c) whitefly vector control (partially effective due to insecticide resistance), (d) tolerant/resistant cassava varieties — KU50 and Huaybong60 consistently show fewer symptomatic plants, lower disease progress, and higher yields in CMD-pressure regions.

causal_species.quote: Sri Lanka Cassava Mosaic Virus (spread via infected cuttings and whiteflies)

description.quote: Sri Lankan cassava mosaic virus (SLCMV) is the primary pathogen responsible for cassava mosaic disease in cassava plantations and is transmitted via infected cutting stems and the whitefly vector, Bemisia tabaci. SLCMV-infected plants exhibit leaf curl and mosaic in young leaves. Popular cassava varieties KU50 and Huaybong60 showed superior CMD tolerance

favored_conditions.quote: Only Asia II 1 whiteflies were able to transmit this virus efficiently among three widely distributed whitefly species in Asia

notes.en: Cool misty up-country conditions with high humidity drive infection. Sunlight limits development — cloudy rainy weather is most dangerous for young flush.

description.en: The most problematic foliar disease of tea in Sri Lanka, attacking young succulent harvestable leaves and causing **~40% yield loss** under pressure. Lesions start as small translucent spots on young leaves, swell into characteristic convex 'blisters' on the lower leaf surface with a corresponding depression above, then produce a velvety white sporulating layer. TRI management programme, built since 1946: (a) copper-based fungicides — the TRI-pioneered Perenox; modern Champ DP with lower metallic copper content (20–37.5%) recommended 2016–2019; (b) systemic fungicides bitertanol, tridemorph, propiconazole (1984 TRI recommendation); (c) non-pesticidal options — sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) and salicylic acid show statistically significant reduction vs. copper; (d) Trichoderma biocontrol isolates under investigation.

description.quote: Blister blight caused by Exobasidium vexans Massee is the most problematic foliar disease in tea in Sri Lanka. The blister blight disease is one such serious foliar tea disease caused by the obligate biotrophic fungus Exobasidium vexans, which primarily infects the young succulent harvestable tea leaves and results in ~40% yield crop loss.

favored_conditions.quote: Recommendation of a copper fungicide (Perenox) against control of blister blight causal agent, Exobasidium vexans (1946). Recommendation of three systemic fungicides (bitertanol, tridermorph, propiconazole) for the control of blister blight (1984). bioefficacy and residue trials for granular and liquid copper formulations with lower copper content (20-37.5% metallic copper) & recommendation of Champ DP to manage Blister blight disease (2016-2019)

notes.en: Mainly attacks old and mature tea leaves; injury from harsh plucking or hail storms creates entry points for spore germination.

description.en: **Yield loss 20–50% in major tea-growing countries including Sri Lanka**. Early symptoms: light yellow-brown spots that progress to brown then grey-white; necrotic spots become irregular/circular/semi-circular, with abundant acervuli (black dots) in rings on the adaxial leaf surface. **Management per TRI Plant Pathology Division's Integrated Disease Management (IDM) approach**: (a) **Fungicides** — Carbendazim + Mancozeb mix, Hexaconazole, Propiconazole, Validamycin (85.1–89.8% inhibition of *P. ampullacea*); (b) **Biological** — *Trichoderma harzianum*, *T. reesei*, *T. hamatum*, *Bacillus subtilis*, *Microbacterium barkeri* (66.6–84.2% antagonistic activity) — alternative to synthetic fungicides aligned with TRI's 'minimal chemicals' cleanliness goal; (c) canopy / plucking-frequency management to avoid stress; (d) rotate fungicide MoA groups.

causal_species.quote: Pseudopestalotiopsis ampullacea as a new pathogen causing tea gray blight

description.quote: Grey blight is estimated to lower the yield by 20% to 50% in major tea growing countries, including China, Japan, India, and Sri Lanka. Early disease symptoms start with light yellow brown spots, which change brown and grey-white with disease progresses. Carbendazim + mancozeb, hexaconazole, propiconazole, and valextra were effective fungicides with an 85.1% to 89.8% range of inhibitory activity against P. ampullacea. Trichoderma harzianum, T. reesei, T. hamatum, Bacillus subtilis, and Microbacterium barkeri were efficient bioagents

notes.en: Transmitted persistently and propagatively by thrips — primarily *Frankliniella occidentalis* and *Thrips tabaci*. Only larval-acquired virus is transmitted by adults. Alternate weed hosts are critical reservoirs.

description.en: Confirmed infecting groundnut and tomato in Sri Lanka, with characteristic tospovirus symptoms — **concentric rings on leaves and fruits** — observed in locations including Ambalantota and Gannoruwa. Young leaves may show bronze discolouration, cup-shaped curling, and black streaking on stems; fruits develop chlorotic ringspots and may abort. Management requires **multiple tactics** because resistance (e.g., Sw-5 gene) alone is defeated by resistance-breaking strains: (a) genetic resistance — deploy resistant cultivars in rotation; (b) strict thrips vector control (monitoring + selective insecticides; avoid broad-spectrum that suppresses natural enemies); (c) **weed management** — weeds host both virus and thrips, so eliminating endemic infection is critical; (d) reflective mulches; (e) remove infected plants promptly.

description.quote: TSWV has been confirmed to infect groundnut and tomato in Sri Lanka, with characteristic tospovirus symptoms such as concentric rings observed on leaves and fruits of tomato in locations including Ambalantota and Gannoruwa

favored_conditions.quote: using a single tactic, even a very effective one like host plant resistance, runs the risk of that tactic failing over time. some strains of TSWV have been discovered to overcome resistance genes, such as the Sw-5 resistance gene in tomato. Weeds play a critical role in TSWV epidemiology by serving as reservoirs for the virus and as reproductive hosts for thrips vectors

causal_species: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (Geminiviridae: Begomovirus)

notes.en: Transmitted persistently by the silverleaf whitefly (*Bemisia tabaci* biotype B). First 5–6 weeks after transplant are the critical protection window. Whitefly inoculates plant within 15 min of feeding after a 24 h latent period.

description.en: Upward curling and yellowing of young leaves, severe stunting, leaf cupping, flower abortion and heavy yield loss in tomato. Among the most economically important tomato diseases globally. Management: destroy crop residue, reflective mulches to repel whiteflies early, plant TYLCV-resistant varieties where available, at-plant + drip + foliar insecticides for vector control, and strict seedling screening (the first 5–6 weeks post-transplant must be protected).

causal_species.quote: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) is one of the most economically important diseases of tomato globally. It is vectored persistently by the silverleaf whitefly, Bemisia tabaci biotype B

favored_conditions.quote: Whiteflies can inoculate a tomato plant with TYLCV after 15 min of feeding. There is a latent period of about 24 h. Protection of the crop from TYLCV during the first 5 or 6 wk after transplanting is crucial to reduce yield losses.

description.quote: viral diseases (TYLCV, CTV, TSWV, CMV)

notes.en: Confined to southern Sri Lanka (Weligama, Matara districts) since 2006. Vector confirmation under CRI study; quarantine zone enforced to prevent spread.

description.en: A devastating phytoplasma disease **first recorded in 2006** in the Weligama area, Southern Province. Infected palms show progressive foliar yellowing, leaflet wilting, reduced nut production, and eventual palm death. Phytoplasmas live protected inside plant phloem cells — no chemical cure. **CRI / CCB / Ministry of Plantation Industries management strategy**: (1) **mandatory rogue-and-burn** of infected palms (only practical suppression tactic); (2) **3 km × 80 km buffer zone** demarcated around infested area with continuous inspection; (3) **statutory quarantine** by gazette — transport of coconut seedlings, fresh nuts, husks, or any palm material from the zone is prohibited by law; (4) **breeding for resistance** — screening of local germplasm identified **Sri Lanka Green Dwarf with 98% resistance**, individual Sri Lanka Yellow Dwarf and Gon Thembili Tall genotypes also resistant, and **Sri Lanka Tall (the dominant commercial variety) highly susceptible**. King coconut identified susceptible. Long-term sustainability depends on rolling out resistant hybrids (Dwarf × Tall) into the southern coconut triangle.

description.quote: Weligama coconut leaf wilt disease (WCLWD) causes heavy losses in the coconut cultivations of southern Sri Lanka. The disease was first reported in 2006 from the Weligama area in the Southern Province. The causal organism of WCLWD is phytoplasma. A 3 km wide and 80 km long buffer zone was marked demarcating the diseased area

favored_conditions.quote: Out of the germplasm evaluated, Sri Lanka Green Dwarf recorded a high level of resistance (98%), while certain individual genotypes within Sri Lanka Yellow dwarf and Gon thembili tall were resistant, and king coconut was identified to be susceptible. The most popular commercial coconut variety, Sri Lanka tall was highly susceptible to the disease.
